lblSubTitle: Heavy transport calculations for road cargo
lblHazard: Slippery, dirty or uncertain surface (Locks µ to 0.2)
modalSubTitle: Tons of cargo per top-over lashing (LC=1600, Stf=400 daN at steep angle)
expDirectTitle: Direct lashing is based on mechanical strength (LC) and angles:
expDirectStep1:
Requirement minus friction: Regulatory requirements are 80% forward, 50% backward/sideways. Remaining force needed:
Forward: 80% - {muPercent}% = {remPercent}% of cargo weight
expDirectStep2:
Angular loss: Calculated based on the chosen angle configuration:
Effective strength per strap: approx {capStrap} tons
expDirectStep3: Symmetry: Always rounded up to the nearest even pair to ensure structural vehicle balance.
expTopOverTitle: Why does it require {total} straps for {weightKg} kg load?
expTopOverStep1:
Friction deduction: Braking requires securing for 80% of forward force. Since bed friction contributes {muPercent}%, only {remPercent}% remains to be secured mechanically:
Force remaining to secure forward = {weightKg} kg × {remDecimal} = {remForceKg} kg
expTopOverStep2:
The EN 12195-1 base table: The official reference matrix assumes a standard strap (Stf = 400 daN) pulled at a steep angle. At friction µ = {mu}, this gives a base capacity of:
Forward capacity (Base): {baseVal} tons ({baseValKg} kg) / strap
expTopOverStep3:
Adjustment for your label (Stf): With your input value of {stf} daN, the baseline modifier is:
{stf} daN ÷ 400 daN = {factor}x power per strap
expTopOverStep4:
Angle check according to TYA rule: You selected {angleRangeText}. {angleExplText}
True capacity per strap forward = {baseVal} tons × {factor} {angleMath} = {trueCap} tons ({trueCapKg} kg)
expTopOverStep5: Final calculation & safety standard: Dividing the remaining demand by your true strap capacity ({remForceKg} kg ÷ {trueCapKg} kg/strap = {exactStraps} straps) mathematically yields {mathStraps} straps. However, the standard mandates a **minimum of 2 straps** for free-standing cargo to prevent rotation!
